script: In 1666, a tiny spark in a baker's shop on Pudding Lane erupted into the Great Fire of London. It raged for four days, devouring 13,200 houses and 87 churches. Incredibly, only six people were confirmed dead. The fire reshaped London forever, leading to brick buildings and the birth of modern fire insurance.

In 1947, Harvard engineers found a moth trapped inside the Mark II computer. They taped it in the logbook with the note 'First actual case of bug being found.' That's how we got the term 'computer bug.' The moth is now preserved in the Smithsonian.

In July 1518, a woman in Strasbourg began dancing uncontrollably in the streets. Within a month, hundreds joined her. Some danced until they collapsed from heart attacks or exhaustion. Doctors called it 'hot blood' and prescribed more dancing. The plague ended as mysteriously as it began.

Cleopatra lived closer to the invention of the iPhone than to the building of the Great Pyramid. The Great Pyramid was built around 2560 BC. Cleopatra was born in 69 BC. That's a gap of nearly 2500 years. The iPhone launched in 2007, less than 2100 years after Cleopatra.

During the War of 1812, British soldiers from Canada captured Fort Shelby in Michigan. Later, a group of Canadians tried to recapture it from Americans. But the fort was already abandoned, so they shot at each other by mistake. It remains the only battle where Canada invaded Canada.

Napoleon once ordered a rabbit hunt for sport. His men gathered thousands of rabbits. But when released, the rabbits didn't run. They charged straight at Napoleon and his men. The rabbits swarmed the emperor's carriage, and Napoleon was forced to flee. It was his most embarrassing military defeat.

In 1814, a massive vat at the Meux Brewery in London burst, releasing over 323,000 gallons of beer. The wave destroyed homes and killed eight people. People died from drowning, injury, and even alcohol poisoning. The brewery was fined, but Parliament ruled it an 'Act of God.'

topic: Oxford University is Older Than the Aztecs
script: Oxford University began teaching in 1096. The Aztec Empire was founded in 1428. That means Oxford is over 300 years older than the Aztecs. When Oxford was already 300 years old, the Aztecs were just building Tenochtitlan, which later became Mexico City.

In 1932, Australia declared war on emus. Farmers complained emus were destroying crops. The military deployed soldiers with machine guns. The emus won. They scattered and outran the bullets. After a month, the army retreated. The emus remain undefeated to this day.

The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 holds the record for the shortest war ever. It lasted just 38 minutes. After the sultan died, a pro-British successor was wanted, but a different sultan took power. The British Navy bombarded the palace for 38 minutes until surrender was declared.

The story of Mozart being poisoned by rival composer Salieri is mostly fiction. They were actually friendly colleagues. Salieri even taught music to Mozart's son. The murder myth comes from a Pushkin play and the film 'Amadeus.' In reality, Mozart likely died from rheumatic fever.

In the summer of 1858, London's sewage problem became so bad that Parliament couldn't function. The smell from the Thames was unbearable. Curtains soaked in bleach were hung in windows. This crisis finally forced London to build a modern sewer system, designed by Joseph Bazalgette.

In 1930, a woman named Mary Christmas sued a department store for breach of contract. She claimed Santa hadn't delivered the gifts she ordered for her children. The judge dismissed the case, ruling that Santa is a 'mythical figure' not subject to contract law. Mary Christmas made headlines worldwide.

The 1904 Olympic marathon in St. Louis was chaos. One runner hitched a ride in a car for 11 miles. Another stopped for a nap. The winner was given rat poison by his trainer as a stimulant. Only 14 of 32 finished. It remains the strangest Olympic race in history.

In 1630s Holland, tulip bulbs became more valuable than gold. A single bulb could cost ten times a skilled worker's annual salary. People mortgaged homes to buy tulips. Then the market crashed overnight, ruining thousands. It was history's first recorded speculative bubble.

Alexander Selkirk, a Scottish sailor, was marooned on a Pacific island in 1704 after arguing with his captain. He survived alone for four years and 4 months, eating goats and reading the Bible. His rescue inspired Daniel Defoe to write 'Robinson Crusoe,' one of the first English novels.

In the 19th century, hat makers used mercury to treat felt. Long exposure caused mercury poisoning, leading to tremors, slurred speech, and hallucinations. This is where the phrase 'mad as a hatter' comes from. The condition was so common it was called 'hatters' shakes.'

topic: Hitler's Nephew in the US Navy
script: William Hitler, Adolf Hitler's nephew, moved to the United States and joined the US Navy after Pearl Harbor. He served honorably as a pharmacist's mate. After the war, he changed his name to William Stuart-Houston. He died in 1987 and is buried in New York.

The Library of Alexandria wasn't destroyed in a single fire. It declined over centuries due to multiple events, including budget cuts, civil wars, and neglect. By the time the Arabs conquered Alexandria in 642 AD, the library had already been gone for centuries. A great loss for humanity.

In 1860, a samurai named Hikozo visited the US as part of a diplomatic mission. He met President James Buchanan and later fought in the Union Army during the Civil War. After the war, he became a US citizen and returned to Japan as an American diplomat. A true samurai of two worlds.
